options.text: $\sqrt{3}$ is irrational, because $3$ is not a perfect square.

options.why: $1.7$ is only a rounded value; $\sqrt{3}=1.732\ldots$ never stops or repeats.

options.text: $\sqrt{3}$ is rational, because any square root can be simplified.
options.why: Only perfect-square roots simplify to a whole number; $3$ is not a perfect square.

options.text: $\sqrt{3}$ is rational, because it can be written as $\frac{3}{1}$.
options.why: $\frac{3}{1}=3$, but $\sqrt{3}\approx1.73$, so this is not equal to $\sqrt{3}$.

questions.solution_text:
$3$ is not a perfect square, so $\sqrt{3}=1.732\ldots$ never stops and never repeats.
It cannot be written as a fraction.
**Irrational.**

questions.question_text: Which of these numbers is rational?

options.why: $50$ is not a perfect square, so $\sqrt{50}$ is irrational.
$5$ is not a perfect square, so $\sqrt{5}$ is irrational.
$\pi$ is a non-terminating, non-recurring decimal, so it is irrational.

questions.solution_text:
$\sqrt{49}=7=\frac{7}{1}$, a fraction of two integers.
A perfect-square root simplifies to a whole number.
$\sqrt{49}$ is **rational**.

options.text: $\pi$ is irrational, because its decimal never stops and never repeats.

options.why: $\frac{22}{7}$ is only an approximation; it does not equal $\pi$ exactly.
$3.14$ is a rounded value; $\pi=3.14159\ldots$ continues without repeating.

options.text: $\pi$ is rational, because it is used in circle formulas.
options.why: Where a number is used has nothing to do with whether it is rational.

questions.solution_text:
$\pi=3.14159\ldots$ is a non-terminating, non-recurring decimal, so it cannot be written as a fraction.
**Irrational.**

questions.question_text: Which of these numbers is irrational?

options.why: $0.\dot{6}=\frac{2}{3}$, a fraction of two integers, so it is rational.
$\frac{22}{7}$ is already a fraction of two integers, so it is rational.
$0.75=\frac{3}{4}$, a terminating decimal, so it is rational.

questions.solution_text:
$0.\dot{6}$, $\frac{22}{7}$ and $0.75$ can all be written as fractions, so they are rational.
$6$ is not a perfect square, so $\sqrt{6}=2.449\ldots$ never stops or repeats.
$\sqrt{6}$ is **irrational**.

questions.question_text: Which list contains only irrational numbers?

options.why: $\sqrt{9}=3$ is rational, so the list is not all irrational.
$0.\dot{3}=\frac{1}{3}$ is rational, so the list is not all irrational.
$\frac{22}{7}$ is a fraction, hence rational, so the list is not all irrational.

questions.solution_text:
$\sqrt{2},\ \pi$ and $\sqrt{5}$ each have non-terminating, non-recurring decimals and cannot be written as fractions.
Every other list contains a perfect-square root, a recurring decimal or a fraction, which are rational.
The list $\sqrt{2},\ \pi,\ \sqrt{5}$ is **all irrational**.

questions.question_text: Dolly writes $\pi=\frac{22}{7}$ and concludes $\pi$ is rational. What is the mistake?

options.text: $\frac{22}{7}$ is only an approximation of $\pi$; $\pi$ cannot be written exactly as a fraction, so it is irrational.
There is no mistake; $\pi$ really does equal $\frac{22}{7}$.
options.why: $\frac{22}{7}=3.142857\ldots$ differs from $\pi=3.14159\ldots$, so they are not equal.

options.text: The mistake is that $\pi$ is actually rational and equals $3.14$.
options.why: $3.14$ is also only an approximation; $\pi$'s digits continue without repeating.

options.text: The mistake is that $\frac{22}{7}$ is irrational.
options.why: $\frac{22}{7}$ is a ratio of integers, so it is rational; the error is claiming it equals $\pi$.

questions.solution_text:
$\frac{22}{7}=3.142857\ldots$ is only a close approximation of $\pi=3.14159\ldots$; they are not equal.
$\pi$ is non-terminating and non-recurring, so it cannot be written as a fraction.
$\pi$ is **irrational**.
